answers.field_label: I Describe My His Her Disability As
answers.value:
My 9-year-old son, Jacob, has an autism spectrum disorder (ASD) diagnosis with co-occurring language delay and sensory processing differences. He was diagnosed around age 3, after we noticed that his speech was delayed, he had a very hard time with changes in routine, and he became easily overwhelmed in noisy or crowded environments. 

In day-to-day life, his disability shows up in a few main ways:
- Communication: He speaks in short sentences and can express basic needs and preferences, but he struggles with back-and-forth conversation, understanding figurative language, and reading social cues. We use visual supports, like picture schedules and written checklists, to help him understand expectations and transitions.
- Sensory processing: Jacob is very sensitive to loud noises and unexpected sounds. Fire alarms, hand dryers in public bathrooms, and crowded cafeterias can be extremely distressing for him. We carry noise-canceling headphones, and we plan our outings to avoid peak crowds when possible. He also seeks certain sensory input, like rocking, jumping, and chewing on chewable jewelry.
- Executive functioning and flexibility: Transitions—like leaving the house, stopping an activity he enjoys, or switching from one task to another—are difficult. He benefits from lots of advance warning and clear visual schedules. Unexpected changes can lead to meltdowns, which are not “tantrums” in the usual sense, but rather his nervous system becoming overwhelmed and him losing the ability to cope.
- Social interaction: He is very kind and curious, but he has a hard time understanding other children’s perspectives and joining in group play. He often prefers parallel play or one-on-one interactions with adults or familiar peers. He can be misunderstood as “not interested,” when really he is anxious and unsure how to join in.

Jacob is also very bright and has a remarkable memory for details. He loves maps, train schedules, and weather patterns. When he is interested in something, he can focus intensely and learn an incredible amount about it. His disability doesn’t define him, but it shapes the support he needs to participate fully at school, in after-school programs, and in the community.

As his parent, I see his disability as a different way of processing the world that comes with both strengths and challenges. The biggest barriers we face are usually not Jacob himself, but systems that are not flexible or inclusive—things like rigid school policies, lack of sensory-friendly spaces, limited respite options, and long waits for specialized services. With the right supports—predictable routines, patient communication, and understanding from others—he thrives and shows how much he has to contribute.
